title: Klinefelter Syndrome
oneLiner: 47,XXY male — common chromosomal disorder causing primary hypogonadism, infertility, and increased long‑term vascular/metabolic risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Venous thromboembolism (PE/DVT)** — Klinefelter confers a 2‑3× increased risk of VTE; any acute dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, or unilateral leg swelling requires immediate D‑dimer/CTPA workup.
- **Mediastinal germ cell tumour** — rare but life‑threatening; look for chest pain, stridor, superior vena cava obstruction in a young male with Klinefelter → urgent CT thorax.
- **Testicular torsion** — Klinefelter testes are small and firm but can still torse; sudden scrotal pain + absent cremasteric reflex = emergency exploration, not routine ultrasound.
- **Hypogonadal crisis** — profound fatigue, hypotension, hyponatraemia rarely from adrenal insufficiency (autoimmune association) — check cortisol before assuming simple hypogonadism.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Small, firm testes (<4 mL) after puberty — the single most consistent finding.
  - Gynaecomastia (often bilateral, pubertal onset).
  - Tall stature with eunuchoid proportions (arm span > height, long legs).
  - Delayed or incomplete puberty.
  - Infertility (azoospermia in most).
  - Learning difficulties / language delay / social awkwardness.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Testicular volume <4 mL (Prader orchidometer) — firm, not tender.
  - Gynaecomastia (palpable breast tissue >2 cm).
  - Reduced facial/body hair, sparse axillary hair.
  - Height >90th centile with lower‑segment > upper‑segment ratio.
  - May have cubitus valgus, clinodactyly, or flat feet.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Primary hypogonadism (other causes):** e.g. anorchia, post‑orchitis, chemotherapy — karyotype differentiates (47,XXY vs 46,XY).
- **Secondary hypogonadism (Kallmann, pituitary lesions):** low LH/FSH + low testosterone vs raised LH/FSH in Klinefelter.
- **Constitutional delay of growth and puberty:** normal testicular growth eventually, bone age delayed, no gynaecomastia.
- **Marfan syndrome:** tall, arachnodactyly, lens dislocation, normal testes, normal karyotype.
- **Splitter:** Testicular volume <4 mL + raised LH/FSH = Klinefelter until proven otherwise. Low LH/FSH = hypothalamic/pituitary cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (if abdominal pain in young male? No — but always in young female of reproductive age if gynaecomastia).
  - D‑dimer if VTE suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  - LH, FSH, total testosterone (morning sample).
  - Karyotype (peripheral blood) — gold standard.
  - Oestradiol (if gynaecomastia prominent).
  - HbA1c, lipids (metabolic screening).
- **Imaging:**
  - Testicular ultrasound: small volume, occasionally microcalcifications, malignancy risk (germ cell tumours).
  - DEXA scan: baseline for osteoporosis (start at age 30–40).
  - Mammography / breast ultrasound if suspicious gynaecomastia (exclude breast cancer).
- **Special tests:**
  - Semen analysis (for infertility assessment).
  - Bone age (if pubertal delay).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Karyotype 47,XXY (or mosaic 46,XY/47,XXY).
  - Clinical suspicion: small testes + raised LH/FSH + low/normal testosterone.
- **Never‑miss test:** **Karyotype** — without it you cannot confirm Klinefelter and may miss mosaicism that alters prognosis/management.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute VTE suspected: start LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily SC) after CTPA confirmation; involve haematology.
  - If testicular torsion: emergency scrotal exploration.
  - If mediastinal mass: CT thorax + oncology referral; avoid blind steroids before tissue diagnosis.
  - If hypogonadal crisis (hyponatraemia, hypotension): IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, then 50 mg IV/IM QDS pending synacthen test.
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Start testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) if confirmed and symptomatic (low T + signs/symptoms). **Do not start TRT until karyotype and baseline LH/FSH/T done.**
  - Arrange referral to endocrinology / reproductive medicine.
  - Screen for metabolic syndrome: fasting glucose, lipids, BP.
  - If gynaecomastia is painful/troubling → consider tamoxifen 20 mg daily (off‑label) after endocrinology input.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Testosterone replacement: aim for mid‑normal adult male T levels.
  - Annual review: BP, BMI, HbA1c, lipids, testosterone levels.
  - DEXA scan Q2‑3 years.
  - Breast awareness/self‑examination; consider mammography if gynaecomastia changes.
  - Fertility counselling: sperm retrieval (TESE) possible in some mosaics.
  - Psychological support / speech therapy if learning difficulties.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for another reason):**
  - Routine VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if weight >50 kg, eGFR >30) — but Klinefelter patients are higher risk; consider extended prophylaxis.
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1.5 mL/kg/h if nil by mouth; otherwise encourage oral intake.
  - Observation: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if on LMWH).
  - Diet: normal unless contraindicated.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents:**
  - Testosterone replacement:
    - Testosterone enanthate (Sustanon 250) 250 mg IM every 2‑4 weeks. *[Check local policy for exact frequency and dose adjustments]*
    - Testosterone gel (Testogel 1% or 1.62%) 50 mg (5 g) applied daily to shoulders/arms. *[Check local policy for titration]*
    - Testosterone undecanoate (Nebido) 1000 mg IM every 10‑14 weeks (deep gluteal injection). *[Check local policy for loading regimen]*
  - Tamoxifen (for persistent gynaecomastia): 20 mg PO daily. *[Off‑label, after specialist review]*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Testosterone buccal or nasal gel.
  - Aromatase inhibitors (e.g. anastrozole) for gynaecomastia — specialist only.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Androgens: contraindicated in prostate or breast carcinoma, severe heart/liver/renal disease. Can exacerbate sleep apnoea. Teratogenic in pregnancy.
  - Tamoxifen: avoid with warfarin (increased INR), SSRIs, and in patients with history of VTE.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No specific dose adjustment for testosterone; use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.
  - Tamoxifen: dose reduction may be needed in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Testosterone — prescribing an incorrect dosing interval, especially Sustanon 250: giving too often (e.g. weekly) can cause polycythaemia, hypertension, and mood swings; too rarely (e.g. 6‑weekly) leads to trough hypogonadism.**
  - **Tamoxifen — not checking prothrombin time in patients on warfarin; VTE risk is already high in Klinefelter.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - VTE (DVT/PE) — up to 2‑3% lifetime risk.
  - Testicular torsion (rare but real).
  - Gynaecomastia pain/infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (azoospermia in >90%).
  - Osteoporosis / osteopenia.
  - Metabolic syndrome: obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension.
  - Breast cancer: 20‑50× higher risk than 46,XY males.
  - Autoimmune disorders: SLE, RA, type 1 DM, hypothyroidism.
  - Learning disabilities / social difficulties.
  - Mediastinal germ cell tumours (rare, but increased incidence).
sections.Prognosis: - **Lifespan is near normal** if metabolic complications are managed. **Main morbidity** is from thromboembolism, diabetes, and breast cancer — but early TRT and surveillance reduce risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** order a karyotype in any male with unexplained small testes plus any one of: tall stature, gynaecomastia, infertility, or developmental delay.
- **DO** start DEXA screening by age 30–40 and repeat Q2‑3 years.
- **DO** ask about and screen for learning difficulties — early referral for speech/language therapy improves social outcome.
- **DO** check LH/FSH before starting TRT — if low, consider secondary hypogonadism, not Klinefelter.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT** giving TRT before karyotype result — you will miss the opportunity to confirm diagnosis and assess mosaicism.
- **NOT** assuming all Klinefelter patients are infertile — mosaics may have sperm; refer early for sperm banking before testicular atrophy becomes irreversible.
- **NOT** ignoring gynaecomastia as ‘just part of syndrome’ — it can hide early breast cancer; examine breast tissue and arrange mammogram if hard, fixed, or asymmetric.
- **NOT** forgetting VTE risk — always use prophylaxis on admission, and avoid tamoxifen without thromboprophylaxis in high‑risk patients.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden onset breathlessness, chest pain, haemoptysis (PE).
  - Unilateral leg swelling/pain (DVT).
  - New breast lump, skin dimpling, nipple discharge.
  - Scrotal pain or swelling (torsion or tumour).
  - Severe headaches or visual disturbance (intracranial germ cell tumour).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine clinic: 2‑4 weeks after starting TRT for symptom review and dosing; then every 6–12 months.
  - DEXA scan and metabolic screening annually (GP can coordinate).
  - Breast surveillance: self‑exam monthly, clinical exam yearly by GP or endocrinology.
  - Fertility clinic if sperm retrieval desired.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / endocrinology registrar if:**
  - Suspected testicular torsion (needs immediate surgery, not ultrasound).
  - Acute chest pain/hypoxia with high clinical probability of PE (CTPA needed, involve respiratory/acute medicine).
  - Hypercalcaemia or new onset diabetes atypical for age (possible autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.5 mmol/L.
  - GCS <13.
  - Stridor or superior vena cava syndrome (mediastinal mass).
  - Massive PE with shock (get echo, thrombolysis team).

rawMarkdown:
## Klinefelter Syndrome
**One-liner:** 47,XXY male — common chromosomal disorder causing primary hypogonadism, infertility, and increased long‑term vascular/metabolic risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Venous thromboembolism (PE/DVT)** — Klinefelter confers a 2‑3× increased risk of VTE; any acute dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, or unilateral leg swelling requires immediate D‑dimer/CTPA workup.
- **Mediastinal germ cell tumour** — rare but life‑threatening; look for chest pain, stridor, superior vena cava obstruction in a young male with Klinefelter → urgent CT thorax.
- **Testicular torsion** — Klinefelter testes are small and firm but can still torse; sudden scrotal pain + absent cremasteric reflex = emergency exploration, not routine ultrasound.
- **Hypogonadal crisis** — profound fatigue, hypotension, hyponatraemia rarely from adrenal insufficiency (autoimmune association) — check cortisol before assuming simple hypogonadism.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Small, firm testes (<4 mL) after puberty — the single most consistent finding.
  - Gynaecomastia (often bilateral, pubertal onset).
  - Tall stature with eunuchoid proportions (arm span > height, long legs).
  - Delayed or incomplete puberty.
  - Infertility (azoospermia in most).
  - Learning difficulties / language delay / social awkwardness.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Testicular volume <4 mL (Prader orchidometer) — firm, not tender.
  - Gynaecomastia (palpable breast tissue >2 cm).
  - Reduced facial/body hair, sparse axillary hair.
  - Height >90th centile with lower‑segment > upper‑segment ratio.
  - May have cubitus valgus, clinodactyly, or flat feet.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Primary hypogonadism (other causes):** e.g. anorchia, post‑orchitis, chemotherapy — karyotype differentiates (47,XXY vs 46,XY).
- **Secondary hypogonadism (Kallmann, pituitary lesions):** low LH/FSH + low testosterone vs raised LH/FSH in Klinefelter.
- **Constitutional delay of growth and puberty:** normal testicular growth eventually, bone age delayed, no gynaecomastia.
- **Marfan syndrome:** tall, arachnodactyly, lens dislocation, normal testes, normal karyotype.
- **Splitter:** Testicular volume <4 mL + raised LH/FSH = Klinefelter until proven otherwise. Low LH/FSH = hypothalamic/pituitary cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine pregnancy test (if abdominal pain in young male? No — but always in young female of reproductive age if gynaecomastia).
  - D‑dimer if VTE suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  - LH, FSH, total testosterone (morning sample).
  - Karyotype (peripheral blood) — gold standard.
  - Oestradiol (if gynaecomastia prominent).
  - HbA1c, lipids (metabolic screening).
- **Imaging:**
  - Testicular ultrasound: small volume, occasionally microcalcifications, malignancy risk (germ cell tumours).
  - DEXA scan: baseline for osteoporosis (start at age 30–40).
  - Mammography / breast ultrasound if suspicious gynaecomastia (exclude breast cancer).
- **Special tests:**
  - Semen analysis (for infertility assessment).
  - Bone age (if pubertal delay).
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Karyotype 47,XXY (or mosaic 46,XY/47,XXY).
  - Clinical suspicion: small testes + raised LH/FSH + low/normal testosterone.
- **Never‑miss test:** **Karyotype** — without it you cannot confirm Klinefelter and may miss mosaicism that alters prognosis/management.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute VTE suspected: start LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg once daily SC) after CTPA confirmation; involve haematology.
  - If testicular torsion: emergency scrotal exploration.
  - If mediastinal mass: CT thorax + oncology referral; avoid blind steroids before tissue diagnosis.
  - If hypogonadal crisis (hyponatraemia, hypotension): IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, then 50 mg IV/IM QDS pending synacthen test.
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  - Start testosterone replacement therapy (TRT) if confirmed and symptomatic (low T + signs/symptoms). **Do not start TRT until karyotype and baseline LH/FSH/T done.**
  - Arrange referral to endocrinology / reproductive medicine.
  - Screen for metabolic syndrome: fasting glucose, lipids, BP.
  - If gynaecomastia is painful/troubling → consider tamoxifen 20 mg daily (off‑label) after endocrinology input.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Testosterone replacement: aim for mid‑normal adult male T levels.
  - Annual review: BP, BMI, HbA1c, lipids, testosterone levels.
  - DEXA scan Q2‑3 years.
  - Breast awareness/self‑examination; consider mammography if gynaecomastia changes.
  - Fertility counselling: sperm retrieval (TESE) possible in some mosaics.
  - Psychological support / speech therapy if learning difficulties.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for another reason):**
  - Routine VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily if weight >50 kg, eGFR >30) — but Klinefelter patients are higher risk; consider extended prophylaxis.
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 1.5 mL/kg/h if nil by mouth; otherwise encourage oral intake.
  - Observation: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if on LMWH).
  - Diet: normal unless contraindicated.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents:**
  - Testosterone replacement:
    - Testosterone enanthate (Sustanon 250) 250 mg IM every 2‑4 weeks. *[Check local policy for exact frequency and dose adjustments]*
    - Testosterone gel (Testogel 1% or 1.62%) 50 mg (5 g) applied daily to shoulders/arms. *[Check local policy for titration]*
    - Testosterone undecanoate (Nebido) 1000 mg IM every 10‑14 weeks (deep gluteal injection). *[Check local policy for loading regimen]*
  - Tamoxifen (for persistent gynaecomastia): 20 mg PO daily. *[Off‑label, after specialist review]*
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Testosterone buccal or nasal gel.
  - Aromatase inhibitors (e.g. anastrozole) for gynaecomastia — specialist only.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Androgens: contraindicated in prostate or breast carcinoma, severe heart/liver/renal disease. Can exacerbate sleep apnoea. Teratogenic in pregnancy.
  - Tamoxifen: avoid with warfarin (increased INR), SSRIs, and in patients with history of VTE.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No specific dose adjustment for testosterone; use with caution in severe hepatic impairment.
  - Tamoxifen: dose reduction may be needed in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Testosterone — prescribing an incorrect dosing interval, especially Sustanon 250: giving too often (e.g. weekly) can cause polycythaemia, hypertension, and mood swings; too rarely (e.g. 6‑weekly) leads to trough hypogonadism.**
  - **Tamoxifen — not checking prothrombin time in patients on warfarin; VTE risk is already high in Klinefelter.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - VTE (DVT/PE) — up to 2‑3% lifetime risk.
  - Testicular torsion (rare but real).
  - Gynaecomastia pain/infection.
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (azoospermia in >90%).
  - Osteoporosis / osteopenia.
  - Metabolic syndrome: obesity, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidaemia, hypertension.
  - Breast cancer: 20‑50× higher risk than 46,XY males.
  - Autoimmune disorders: SLE, RA, type 1 DM, hypothyroidism.
  - Learning disabilities / social difficulties.
  - Mediastinal germ cell tumours (rare, but increased incidence).

### Prognosis
- **Lifespan is near normal** if metabolic complications are managed. **Main morbidity** is from thromboembolism, diabetes, and breast cancer — but early TRT and surveillance reduce risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** order a karyotype in any male with unexplained small testes plus any one of: tall stature, gynaecomastia, infertility, or developmental delay.
- **DO** start DEXA screening by age 30–40 and repeat Q2‑3 years.
- **DO** ask about and screen for learning difficulties — early referral for speech/language therapy improves social outcome.
- **DO** check LH/FSH before starting TRT — if low, consider secondary hypogonadism, not Klinefelter.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT** giving TRT before karyotype result — you will miss the opportunity to confirm diagnosis and assess mosaicism.
- **NOT** assuming all Klinefelter patients are infertile — mosaics may have sperm; refer early for sperm banking before testicular atrophy becomes irreversible.
- **NOT** ignoring gynaecomastia as ‘just part of syndrome’ — it can hide early breast cancer; examine breast tissue and arrange mammogram if hard, fixed, or asymmetric.
- **NOT** forgetting VTE risk — always use prophylaxis on admission, and avoid tamoxifen without thromboprophylaxis in high‑risk patients.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Sudden onset breathlessness, chest pain, haemoptysis (PE).
  - Unilateral leg swelling/pain (DVT).
  - New breast lump, skin dimpling, nipple discharge.
  - Scrotal pain or swelling (torsion or tumour).
  - Severe headaches or visual disturbance (intracranial germ cell tumour).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine clinic: 2‑4 weeks after starting TRT for symptom review and dosing; then every 6–12 months.
  - DEXA scan and metabolic screening annually (GP can coordinate).
  - Breast surveillance: self‑exam monthly, clinical exam yearly by GP or endocrinology.
  - Fertility clinic if sperm retrieval desired.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / endocrinology registrar if:**
  - Suspected testicular torsion (needs immediate surgery, not ultrasound).
  - Acute chest pain/hypoxia with high clinical probability of PE (CTPA needed, involve respiratory/acute medicine).
  - Hypercalcaemia or new onset diabetes atypical for age (possible autoimmune polyendocrine syndrome).
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.5 mmol/L.
  - GCS <13.
  - Stridor or superior vena cava syndrome (mediastinal mass).
  - Massive PE with shock (get echo, thrombolysis team).
